caveats: a: The occupation shares are a secondary presentation attributed to BLS and may contain classification overlap or rounding.
a: O*NET occupations include lender, servicing, collection, and underwriting roles outside independent brokerage, so task weights are not observed specifically for NAICS 522310.

a: The injected compensation-to-receipts input uses 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, is harmonized from an establishment-based measure to the screen basis, and therefore carries a stated vintage and basis mismatch.
rho: The key adoption survey is from 2023 and covers mortgage lenders of multiple types, not the screened LMM broker population.
rho: Digital underwriting savings are not identical to generative-AI labor substitution and include process redesign and non-AI automation.
rho: Nonmortgage brokerage may face different data availability and regulation than residential mortgage brokerage.
e: No source directly measures eligibility among firms in the $1-10M normalized EBITDA band.

e: The frozen LMM firm count is itself an ESTIMATE bridged from SUSB size classes using a sector EBITDA margin, so its population may include firms outside the economic band despite the eligibility haircut.
e: Mortgage and nonmortgage brokers share an intermediary model but differ in licensing, borrower type, lender panels, and owner dependence.
s5: Gallup measures plans, not completed transfers, and is not industry-specific.
s5: The mortgage deal tally is not restricted to NAICS 522310 or the LMM band and includes non-control activity.
s5: Private transactions and asset sales may not be publicly disclosed.
q: There is no observed industry measure of AI benefit retention or contract repricing.
q: Residential mortgage compensation rules do not govern every nonmortgage brokerage transaction.
q: Freddie Mac savings are measured for lenders using its tools and can accrue partly to borrowers or counterparties rather than brokers.
d5: Fannie Mae forecasts only through 2027, so years two through five are bounded judgment.
d5: The forecast is residential mortgage-focused, while NAICS 522310 includes nonmortgage brokers.
d5: A dollar origination forecast is not a constant-price quantity measure; home-sales units are the cleaner but incomplete anchor.
o: The consumer evidence covers recent residential homebuyers, not refinances, commercial loans, or other nonmortgage borrowers.
o: Online-only does not necessarily mean operator-free, and hybrid use does not prove that every personal-touch step remains economically necessary.
o: UWM is a large interested market participant and its statements about channel growth and broker value are management views.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Transaction-based commissions or fees paid by borrowers or lenders for loan sourcing, advice, application assembly, lender matching, and closing coordination; repeat volume is driven by referrals, borrower reuse, and continuing lender relationships.
lens.excluded_activities: Direct lenders, loan servicers, captive internal origination units, shells, non-control financing vehicles, and firms whose operations cannot continue independently of the owner.
lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market firms that repeatedly arrange mortgage or nonmortgage loans for external borrowers by matching them with lenders and earning commissions or fees.

a.bridge: The industry workforce proxy reports 42% office/administrative support, 26% business/financial operations, 13% sales, 11% management, and 7% computer/mathematical occupations; O*NET task lists show that loan staff verify, assemble, record, calculate, prepare, and review documents while also advising customers and resolving judgment-heavy cases. I applied higher exposure to document/data work, moderate exposure to analysis and sales support, and low exposure to management and accountable advice, then reduced the result for already-automated rules-based workflows documented by UWM and Freddie Mac. The proxy may overstate remaining exposure where digital underwriting is already mature and understate it where small brokers still handle documents manually.
caveats: The occupation shares are a secondary presentation attributed to BLS and may contain classification overlap or rounding.
O*NET occupations include lender, servicing, collection, and underwriting roles outside independent brokerage, so task weights are not observed specifically for NAICS 522310.

The injected compensation-to-receipts input uses 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, is harmonized from an establishment-based measure to the screen basis, and therefore carries a stated vintage and basis mismatch.
a.change_evidence: Obtain payroll by SOC and time-on-task data from a representative sample of independent mortgage and commercial-loan brokers, segmented by loan type and current LOS/AUS usage; map task minutes before and after deployed AI tools and validate against quality-control exceptions.
a.rationale: Most compensation sits in occupations with large amounts of structured information intake, document reconciliation, status communication, compliance checking, and file preparation. Those tasks are exposed to extraction, drafting, anomaly detection, workflow agents, and avoided incremental hiring, while borrower advice, exception handling, licensing accountability, relationship selling, and final quality control remain less substitutable.

d5.bridge: Fannie Mae forecasts total US home sales rising from 4.814 million in 2026 to 5.131 million in 2027 and single-family mortgage originations from $2.345 trillion to $2.465 trillion. I use housing transaction quantity as the primary mortgage-broker demand proxy, treat the one-year rebound as partly cyclical rather than compounding it, and extend to a five-year constant-price, constant-quality ratio with a wide range. Home sales omit refinances and nonmortgage loans, while origination dollars embed home-price and loan-size changes; these omissions can bias the proxy in either direction.
caveats: Fannie Mae forecasts only through 2027, so years two through five are bounded judgment.
The forecast is residential mortgage-focused, while NAICS 522310 includes nonmortgage brokers.
A dollar origination forecast is not a constant-price quantity measure; home-sales units are the cleaner but incomplete anchor.
d5.change_evidence: Obtain a five-year unit forecast for purchase, refinance, commercial, consumer, and other brokered loans from MBA or another loan-level provider, separate broker-channel share from total originations, and deflate any dollar series by loan-size and service-quality mix.
d5.rationale: The current service basket is highly cyclical, but the near-term forecast indicates recovery rather than structural disappearance, and purchase originations are less rate-sensitive than refinancing. A modest five-year quantity increase is more defensible than extrapolating the forecast rebound, with downside for persistent affordability and rate lock-in and upside for normalization of turnover and refinancing.

caveats: No source directly measures eligibility among firms in the $1-10M normalized EBITDA band.

The frozen LMM firm count is itself an ESTIMATE bridged from SUSB size classes using a sector EBITDA margin, so its population may include firms outside the economic band despite the eligibility haircut.
Mortgage and nonmortgage brokers share an intermediary model but differ in licensing, borrower type, lender panels, and owner dependence.
e.change_evidence: Acquire NMLS company-level roster and call-report data, join to ownership, employee, branch, origination-volume, license-status, and lender-approval records, then sample LMM firms for owner dependence, repeat/referral concentration, and change-of-control portability.
e.rationale: The NAICS definition is intrinsically an external commission-or-fee service, and a major wholesale lender reports relationships with more than 13,000 independent broker businesses, so most operating firms fit the outsourced-service lens. The estimate removes shells, effectively solo books with nontransferable personal goodwill, captive or misclassified units, and firms lacking durable compliance, lender, referral, or staff infrastructure after a change of control.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 522310 ($9,573,018,034) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 522310 ($18,330,974,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.2102 (margins.json[52]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Financial Svcs. (Non-bank & Insurance) EBITDA/Sales 21.02%. Note: Damodaran reports 0.00% for banks (EBITDA not meaningful); bank codes inherit this non-bank default and are flagged ESTIMATE like all n_band values.); revenue/employee $241,188 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 522310); class <5: 7,600 firms, avg revenue/firm $367,148 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $77,175 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 1,106 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,248,567 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $262,449 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 479 firms, avg revenue/firm $2,970,474 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $624,394 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 293 firms, avg revenue/firm $7,341,379 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,543,158 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 90 firms, avg revenue/firm $40,031,156 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $8,414,549 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 63 firms, avg revenue/firm $110,842,032 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $23,298,995 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 383

o.bridge: Fannie Mae reports that most recent homebuyers used a hybrid online and personal-touch mortgage process; online-only use was 14% for ages 18-34, 11% for ages 35-44, 13% for ages 45-64, and 3% for ages 65 and older. UWM describes brokers as responsible for advice, initial credit checks, information gathering, and application submission, and reports that roughly two of every ten loans currently use the wholesale channel. I project greater automation and self-service over five years but retain an accountable operator for regulated advice, exceptions, lender matching, disclosures, fraud/compliance escalation, and closing coordination. The borrower survey may understate future software substitution, while current wholesale share is a channel measure rather than the survival rate of today's brokered quantity.
caveats: The consumer evidence covers recent residential homebuyers, not refinances, commercial loans, or other nonmortgage borrowers.
Online-only does not necessarily mean operator-free, and hybrid use does not prove that every personal-touch step remains economically necessary.
UWM is a large interested market participant and its statements about channel growth and broker value are management views.
o.change_evidence: Track NSMO channel-use and online-only responses through 2031, combine them with HMDA wholesale-channel originations and NMLS broker counts, and interview wholesale lenders on files completed without broker human intervention, exception rates, and accountability allocation.
o.rationale: Digital intake and verification will absorb more steps, yet current consumers predominantly combine digital tools with personal contact and the workflow still assigns consequential, licensed, and exception-heavy duties to people. Software is more likely to compress labor per file and change the service interface than to eliminate the accountable brokerage operator from most brokered transactions within five years.

caveats: There is no observed industry measure of AI benefit retention or contract repricing.
Residential mortgage compensation rules do not govern every nonmortgage brokerage transaction.
Freddie Mac savings are measured for lenders using its tools and can accrue partly to borrowers or counterparties rather than brokers.
q.change_evidence: Collect five years of loan-level broker compensation, fee-waiver, lender-paid pricing, customer acquisition cost, pull-through, and contribution-margin data from acquired firms and compare cohorts before and after workflow automation, controlling for loan size, product, rate cycle, and channel competition.
q.rationale: Broker revenue is transaction-based rather than cost-plus, so labor savings are not mechanically owed to customers. Retention is nevertheless limited by transparent closing disclosures, lender-paid versus borrower-paid compensation rules, wholesale-lender and broker competition, renewal-less transaction repricing, and the likelihood that operators reinvest part of savings in faster service, compliance, lead generation, or consumer pricing. The range represents the discounted five-year share of implemented gross benefit retained after those leakages.

rho.bridge: Fannie Mae surveyed mortgage lenders rather than independent brokers: 7% reported deployed AI/ML, 22% limited or trial deployment, and 29% expected broader rollout within two years in 2023. Freddie Mac separately documents material savings from digital underwriting tools and a KeyBank implementation in under a year. I extend those adoption signals to a five-year broker implementation share while discounting for smaller-firm integration capacity, licensing, fair-lending, privacy, auditability, and human-review requirements. The lender sample likely overstates small-broker implementation resources but may understate the speed of vendor-mediated adoption.
caveats: The key adoption survey is from 2023 and covers mortgage lenders of multiple types, not the screened LMM broker population.
Digital underwriting savings are not identical to generative-AI labor substitution and include process redesign and non-AI automation.
Nonmortgage brokerage may face different data availability and regulation than residential mortgage brokerage.
rho.change_evidence: Run a 2026-2027 survey of NMLS-licensed broker owners by employee count covering production use, share of files touched, straight-through processing, human override rates, audit findings, integration costs, and planned deployment dates.
rho.rationale: Proven digital underwriting economics and vendor-embedded tools should move more than half of exposed work into production over five years, but measured adoption was still limited and cited integration complexity, unproven results, cost, data security, and privacy as barriers. Implementation therefore trails task exposure materially.

s5.bridge: Gallup found 22% of US employer-business owners planned to sell or transfer ownership within five years, but that population spans all industries and includes gifts and rare public offerings. I reduce it for plans that will not close, internal restructurings, untransferable owner books, and nonqualifying outcomes, while mortgage-sector reporting of 62 broadly defined transactions and strategic events in 2025 supports an active buyer environment. The broad owner survey may overstate completed qualifying transfers; the public mortgage deal count understates private deals but overstates relevance because it includes lenders, servicers, technology, title, appraisal, investments, joint ventures, exits, and entrances.
caveats: Gallup measures plans, not completed transfers, and is not industry-specific.
The mortgage deal tally is not restricted to NAICS 522310 or the LMM band and includes non-control activity.
Private transactions and asset sales may not be publicly disclosed.
s5.change_evidence: Build a five-year cohort of NMLS company ownership and control-person changes, match it to state change-of-control approvals and PitchBook or S&P Capital IQ deal records, and manually classify sales, gifts, internal reorganizations, closures, and continued operations for LMM firms.
s5.rationale: Employer firms have meaningful stated transfer intent, and mortgage-market consolidation provides a real path to control transactions. Conversion from intent to a qualifying closed transfer is lower because many small brokerages are owner-led, licenses and lender relationships require continuity work, and some stressed firms close instead of selling.

principal_driver: Document-intensive loan workflows create meaningful AI-enabled capacity gains while licensed brokers remain the accountable interface for complex, regulated transactions.
principal_weakness: Revenue and enterprise value can be tied to volatile transaction volumes and owner-producer relationships that may not survive a control transfer.

caveats: The a estimate rests on a secondary occupation-mix presentation and O*NET occupations that extend beyond independent brokerage; no NAICS 522310 payroll-by-task or time-on-task evidence is available.

The rho bridge extrapolates 2023 lender adoption and lender digital-underwriting examples to a 55% five-year implementation share for smaller brokers; broker-specific production adoption, file-touch, and override rates are unobserved.
The e estimate has no direct evidence for eligibility or transferability among firms in the $1-10M normalized-EBITDA band, and the injected firm count is itself a margin-bridged estimate.

The s5 proxy combines cross-industry owner intentions with a broad mortgage event tally that includes non-control and non-broker activity; neither source measures completed qualifying broker transfers.

The q range is an honest estimate rather than an observed retention measure; residential compensation rules and lender savings do not establish how much automation benefit an acquired broker retains across mortgage and nonmortgage business.
The d5 base extends a residential forecast that ends in 2027 through a five-year horizon and lacks a unit forecast for refinance, commercial, consumer, and other brokered loans.

The o bridge maps recent-homebuyer channel behavior and interested-party wholesale-lender evidence to future operator necessity; hybrid interaction and wholesale share do not directly measure transactions that economically require a human broker.

sources_audited.note: The Census page defines NAICS 522310 as arranging loans by bringing borrowers and lenders together on a commission or fee basis and expressly distinguishes direct real-estate lending and loan servicing.
The page reports the cited 42%, 26%, 13%, 11%, and 7% major occupation shares and attributes the workforce table to BLS; it remains a secondary presentation rather than broker payroll microdata.
O*NET lists verification, document assembly, computer recordkeeping, underwriting submission, customer contact, and related loan-processing tasks as stated.
O*NET states that loan officers evaluate or recommend approval and advise borrowers, and it includes mortgage loan officers and agents in the occupation.

The 2023 Fannie Mae lender survey supports the 7% deployed, 22% limited/trial, 29% expected broader rollout, and cited barriers and use cases, but it does not directly support a five-year implementation share for independent LMM brokers.

Freddie Mac supports the cited lender savings, five-day timeline reduction, defect reduction, automated underwriting functions, and KeyBank implementation timing, but these lender digital-capability results are not broker-specific generative-AI substitution evidence.

Regulation Z supports restrictions on transaction-term-based loan-originator compensation and dual compensation for dwelling-secured consumer credit, but it neither measures benefit retention nor covers all nonmortgage brokerage.

Gallup supports the 22% five-year sell, transfer, or public-offering intent among employer-business owners and the fall 2024 survey timing, but plans across all industries are not completed qualifying broker transfers.

HousingWire reports 62 tracked events in 2025 versus 37 in 2024 and discloses broad subsector and transaction-type coverage; this supports general mortgage-market activity but not an NAICS 522310 LMM control-transfer rate.

The June 2026 forecast supports the exact 2026 and 2027 home-sales and single-family origination figures, but it ends in 2027, is residential-focused, and does not itself support the five-year constant-quantity extension.

Fannie Mae supports predominantly hybrid recent-homebuyer behavior and the cited age-group online-only percentages, but channel preference does not directly measure the future operator-required share of brokered quantity.

The 2025 UWM 10-K supports the 13,000-plus broker relationships, roughly 20% wholesale-channel share, broker workflow, deployed automation, and lower historical purchase-volume volatility; several forward-looking channel and durability statements are management views from a large residential wholesale lender.

sources.supports: Defines 522310 as establishments arranging loans by bringing borrowers and lenders together on a commission or fee basis and excludes direct real-estate lending and loan servicing.
sources.title: North American Industry Classification System: Sector 52, NAICS 522310

sources.supports: Reports an industry workforce mix of 42% office and administrative support, 26% business and financial operations, 13% sales, 11% management, and 7% computer and mathematical occupations, attributed to BLS.
sources.title: 2026 Industry Statistics - Mortgage and Nonmortgage Loan Brokers

sources.supports: Lists loan-work tasks including document verification and preparation, records, calculations, application intake, underwriting submission, customer contact, and closings.
sources.title: O*NET OnLine: Loan Interviewers and Clerks

sources.supports: Describes loan officers as evaluating or recommending approval of loans and advising borrowers; includes mortgage loan officers and agents.
sources.title: O*NET OnLine: Loan Officers

sources.supports: Reports 2023 lender AI/ML familiarity and adoption: 65% familiar, 7% deployed, 22% limited or trial deployment, and 29% expecting broader rollout within two years; identifies integration, proof, cost, security, and privacy barriers and relevant use cases.
sources.title: Mortgage Lenders Cite Operational Efficiency as Primary Motivation for AI Adoption

sources.supports: Reports up to $1,700 per-loan savings from LPA digital capabilities, five-day shorter timelines, lower defects, and a KeyBank implementation in under a year, while documenting automation of underwriting and verification tasks.
sources.title: 2025 Updates to the Cost to Originate Study

sources.supports: Documents restrictions on transaction-term-based compensation and dual compensation, including the borrower-paid versus creditor-paid structure for mortgage brokers.
sources.title: Regulation Z Section 1026.36: Loan Originator Compensation and Requirements

sources.supports: Reports that 22% of US employer-business owners planned to sell or transfer ownership within five years, compared with 9% of nonemployers, based on a fall 2024 survey.
sources.title: Most Small-Business Owners Lack a Succession Plan

sources.supports: Reports 62 publicly tracked mortgage-sector M&A, exit/entry, investment, and joint-venture events in 2025 versus 37 in 2024, while noting private activity is underreported and scope includes multiple mortgage subsectors.
sources.title: Mortgage M&A Poised to Accelerate in 2026

sources.supports: Forecasts total home sales of 4.814 million in 2026 and 5.131 million in 2027, and single-family mortgage originations of $2.345 trillion and $2.465 trillion, respectively.
sources.title: Fannie Mae Housing Forecast: June 2026

sources.supports: Finds most recent homebuyers used hybrid online and personal-touch channels; reports online-only use of 14%, 11%, 13%, and 3% across successive adult age groups and documents growing digital preferences.
sources.title: Opportunities Exist to Offer Digital Verification Tools to More Homebuyers

sources.supports: Reports relationships with over 13,000 independent broker businesses and about 57,000 associated loan officers, wholesale channel share near 20%, broker workflow responsibilities, current automation, and purchase-origination durability.
sources.title: UWM Holdings Corporation 2025 Form 10-K

title: Mortgage and Nonmortgage Loan Brokers
